data.title: Fitch Ratings warns that Southwest Airlines' new baggage fee policy could backfire
data.description:
Fitch Ratings lowered its outlook on Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) to Negative from Stable. The rating agency said the cut reflects concerns over Southwest’s potential shift toward a less conservative financial policy and the risks posed by recent strategic changes, such as introducing bag fees and expiring flight credits. 

Based on Fitch's analysis, the moves, aimed at boosting profitability, could erode Southwest's (NYSE:LUV) competitive strengths relative to network carriers. Fitch also warned that failure to achieve margin improvements or increased leverage due to higher capital deployment could prompt a downgrade.

Fitch said it expects some margin recovery from current low levels, though the ultimate impact of strategic changes remains uncertain. The airline estimates operating profits could exceed $4 billion by 2026, but Fitch adopts a more conservative forecast due to potential customer pushback and economic uncertainties.

On a broad view, Fitch said the evolution of Southwest's (LUV) financial policy is a key concern. Recent developments, including a new CFO appointment, board influence from activist investors, and an accelerated share repurchase program, were highlighted as suggesting a shift toward a less cautious approach. While Southwest (LUV) remains committed to maintaining an investment-grade rating, its flexibility on leverage targets could raise questions with the rating agency about future financial discipline.

On the macro front, economic headwinds add further uncertainty for the airline company. Slower consumer demand and reduced government spending may challenge Southwest's (LUV) efforts to restore profitability. Fitch views Southwest's (LUV) fleet monetization as credit-positive if used for debt reduction or capital costs but warns against excessive shareholder returns or asset depletion.

Shares of Southwest Airlines (LUV) were down 8.8% in afternoon trading on Thursday amid a broad market decline tied to concerns over tariffs and recession risk.

data.title: W.W. Grainger downgraded at Wolfe Research on tariffs, slowing momentum
data.description:
W.W. Grainger (NYSE:GWW) on Thursday was downgraded to Underperform from a previous investment rating of Peer Perform by analysts at Wolfe Research. They said the industrial supplier faces a "tough setup" heading into its first-quarter earnings report and a challenging path forward under the new U.S. tariff regime.

“While the stock has traded heavy in recent weeks, we are growing increasingly cautious on the FY25 guidance framework, which embeds no impact for tariffs,” Ryan Cooke, analyst at Wolfe, said an April 3 report.

He said Grainger (NYSE:GWW) has 35% to 40% import exposure on U.S. cost of goods sold, and believes that the newly imposed tariffs could compress margins, pushing earnings toward the low end of the company’s fiscal 2025 guidance range. Wolfe’s own EPS forecast now sits at $39.83, below the mid-range of management’s $39 to $41.50 guidance and implying 2% to 3% downside to consensus expectations over the rest of the year.

Pricing, private labels and policy risk

One key concern is the timing and cadence of Grainger’s (GWW) price increases, which are scheduled three times annually. With the next round set for May 1, the company will need to carefully calibrate price hikes against a rapidly evolving economic backdrop.

Grainger’s (GWW) reliance on private-label products, which carry higher margins but are more import-dependent, also poses a risk. These products represent roughly 20% of total sales and earn 10 percentage points higher gross margins than branded goods. In a tariff-heavy environment, these goods may become less price-competitive, impacting both sales and profitability.

Cooke also questioned the sustainability of Grainger’s (GWW) long-term goal to outgrow the market by 400 to 500 basis points, a target set in 2022 when the company benefited from pandemic-driven supply chain advantages and strong e-commerce demand. With competitive dynamics normalizing and the company missing the mark in each of the last three quarters, Wolfe sees further downside risk.

Additionally, Cooke flagged government-related exposure — particularly from the Department of Government Efficiency, or DOGE — as another vulnerability. Government entities make up about 16% of Grainger’s (GWW) sales, primarily at the state and local level, but budget constraints could disproportionately affect the company compared to peers.

Valuation not compelling

While Grainger’s (GWW) valuation has moderated in recent months, Cooke noted that the stock still trades at roughly 24 times next-twelve-months price-to-earnings ratio, above its five-year median of about 20 times. Wolfe’s fair value estimate implies 4% downside, but Cooke warned that the risk could exceed 20% if the stock reverts to historical multiples in the high teens.

“Bottom line, there are many hurdles to overcome and risk/reward is heavily out of kilter from here,” Cooke concluded.

Wolfe also upgraded Fastenal (FAST) to Peer Perform, citing a more balanced risk/reward profile relative to Grainger (GWW). The researcher downgraded Parker-Hannifin (PH) and Hayward Holdings (HAYW) to Peer Perform.

data.title: Nasdaq-100 plunges 5% and these 10 holdings have fallen the hardest
data.description:
Trump's latest tariff announcement has sent tremors through the market, triggering a significant sell-off and pushing the Nasdaq-100 (NDX) into a steep decline, with the index dropping 5% on Thursday. The tech-heavy index has suffered broad losses, with 80 of its 100 constituent stocks trading in the red. 

A major contributor to the downturn has been Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP), whose shares have plummeted 16.9% on the day. Below are the top 10 worst-performing Nasdaq-100 stocks of the day.

No. 10: Atlassian Corporation (NASDAQ:TEAM) -10.8%.

No. 9: AppLovin Corporation (NASDAQ:APP) -10.9%.

No. 8: Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) -11.2%.

No. 7: ON Semiconductor Corporation (NASDAQ:ON) -11.3%.

No. 6: MicroStrategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) -11.5%.

No. 5: Dianmondback Energy (NASDAQ:FANG) -11.8%.

No. 4:Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) -12.1%.

No. 3: The Trade Desk (NASDAQ:TTD) -12.3%.

No. 2: Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) -14.4%.

No. 1: Microchip Technology (NASDAQ:MCHP) -16.9%.

Prices are reflected as of 2:30 pm EST.

Moreover, see how Seeking Alpha’s quant grades list the 10 above names: TEAM 3.34, APP 3.49, SBUX 3.20, ON 2.64, MSTR 3.32, FANG 3.15, WBD 3.44, TTD 1.39, MU 4.31, and MCHP 1.65.

As the Nasdaq-100 tumbles, so too does Wall Street's fifth-largest exchange-traded fund. The Invesco QQQ Trust ETF (NASDAQ:QQQ), with $299.80B in assets under management, has also fallen by 4.9%. 

Furthermore, see some other Nasdaq-100 (NDX) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) focused exchange traded funds: (NASDAQ:QQQM), (NYSEARCA:QLD), (NASDAQ:TQQQ), (NYSEARCA:QID), and (NASDAQ:SQQQ).

data.title: UnitedHealth must face Medicare fraud case: DoJ says
data.description:
The U.S. Department of Justice ((DoJ)) has urged a federal judge to proceed with a long-running fraud lawsuit alleging that UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) overbilled the Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) by at least $2.1B.

The DOJ’s proposal came at a federal court on Wednesday after a special master assigned to the case argued in March that the agency lacked evidence to back up its fraud allegations. UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) had until Apr. 2 to respond.

The company is expected to respond to the DOJ by May 2 as the case, initially filed by whistleblower Benjamin Poehling, a former UNH staffer, more than a decade ago, continues.

In 2017, the DoJ took over the case, which alleges that UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), the leading player in the market for government-backed Medicare Advantage plans, failed to delete unsupported diagnosis codes from patient records, leading to as much as $2.1B in overpayments for its MA plans.

data.title: Trump’s tariffs are not priced in, inflation could rise to 5% – UBS
data.description:
UBS’s Tariff Fear gauge, which is measured on a scale of 0-100, recorded 11%, down from 30% in March, for U.S. equities (SP500), meaning tariffs could not be priced in, according to UBS.

European equities (VGK), (IEV), (FEZ) were at 46% this month as of Wednesday.

This fear gauge calculates returns of U.S. and European tariff-affected companies, not explained by changes in macro variables such as growth, real rates, credit spreads or inflation expectations, UBS analysts explained.

“Essentially, until yesterday, the markets were thinking of tariffs purely as a negotiating tool, not an ideological one,” said Bhanu Baweja, chief strategist at UBS. “Indeed, there is a chance that rates of reciprocal tariffs do come lower as trading partners negotiate with the U.S., but the 10% universal tariff suggests this administration firmly believes tariffs are the policy needed to fundamentally alter trade and economic relationships with the global economy.”

He added that the hope for these tariffs being negotiated down are immediately challenged by the possibility of retaliation from the U.S.’s trading partners and how difficult it is for countries to negotiate lowering other non-tariff barriers, including domestic taxes, currency levels and other regulations.

“Trump's proposals are likely to raise the weighted average tariffs on U.S. imports from 2.5% as of end 2024 to 24% – levels not seen since the 1920s,” Baweja said. “Both the magnitude of ‘reciprocal’ tariffs imposed per country and the set of countries they were imposed on were larger than many had reasonably anticipated.”

The broad 10% tariffs imposition has “the potential to considerably worsen the growth inflation mix in the U.S. and the global economy in the coming year,” he said.

UBS economic team projects the U.S.’s 2025 real GDP to lower by 1.5-2 percentage points and inflation to reach 5% if these tariffs are not reversed soon.

“The magnitude of damage they could cause to the U.S. economy makes one’s rational mind regard the possibility of them sticking as low.”

data.title: Enbridge, MPLX to partner with Targa on Traverse Pipeline final investment decision
data.description:
Infrastructure company WhiteWater said Thursday its WPC joint venture with Enbridge (ENB) and MPLX (NYSE:MPLX) has partnered with Targa Resources (TRGP) to reach a final investment decision for the construction of the Traverse pipeline, after securing sufficient firm transportation agreements with investment grade shippers.

The Traverse pipeline is designed to transport as much as 1.75B cf/day of natural gas through ~160 miles of 36-inch pipeline along the Gulf Coast between Agua Dulce and Katy in Texas.

Traverse will be wholly owned by the Blackcomb Pipeline joint venture, which is owned 70% by WPC, 17.5% by Targa (TRGP), and 12.5% by MPLX (NYSE:MPLX); it will be built and operated by WhiteWater and is expected to be in service by 2027.

"The Traverse pipeline enhances optionality for shippers to access multiple premium markets," the companies said.

data.title: March nonfarms payroll to provide hard data in sea of uncertainty
data.description:
As the Trump administration barrelled ahead with a sweeping round of tariffs on Wednesday, roiling markets, the U.S. economy plugs along. The next big economic checkpoint is the Department of Labor's March employment report.

Nonfarm payrolls are expected to add 131K jobs during the month, according to a consensus of economists, slowing from the 151K jobs created in February. The unemployment rate is expected to edge up to 4.2% from 4.1% in the previous month.

Average hourly earnings are expected to rise 0.3% M/M, unchanged from February. Likewise, the year-over-year increase in AHE is expected to stay at 4.0%.

Citi expects that the U.S. economy added 95K jobs in March, a shift from the average three-month pace of well over 100K per month. 

"Given an already-shaky backdrop this week around additional tariff announcements, the realization of softer labor market data could be a particularly large risk-off catalyst," Citi's Veronica Clark wrote in a note to clients, as she expects weak hiring in March to limit job growth.

She noted that market expectations have shifted more negative due to weaker consumer spending, cuts to government funding and jobs, and rising tariffs.

Daniel Doderer, an economist and research director at Flack Global Metals, also sees potential for a downside surprise. He's expecting an increase of 85K-95K jobs during the month.

Both Clark and Doderer noted that federal job cuts will start to show up in the March jobs report.

However, federal government job cuts aren't as straight-forward as one might expect. Many federal employees were laid off in mid-February, which would normally show up in the March payrolls. But "a substantial share" of those workers were rehired and received back pay for their time off, Clark said. Rather, she expects the impact of federal job cuts to become more noticeable in the May-to-September timeframe.

The Fed and economists will also closely watch average hourly earnings growth. Citi's Clark estimates that AHE grew 0.2% M/M in March, less than the 0.3% consensus, as she expects weakening labor demand to weigh on wage growth.

Like the consensus, Citi expects the unemployment rate to rise to 4.2%, but Doderer expects that it will stay at 4.1%. He sees labor supply growth staying low as immigration stays severely restricted and tempers any upward pressure on unemployment.

As for specific sectors, Doderer will be closely watching manufacturing and construction, as they'll be the most affected by immigration. "We're going to be curious to see just how much of an impact that February figure on border occurrences (a record low) did have in overall payrolls numbers," he said.

And while the March data is important, "I think we're going to see some wide swings in revisions over the next couple of months as well," Flack Metal's Doderer said.  "So, putting a lot of weight on an initial reading is not necessarily going to be a good way to operate over the next couple of months."

As for trends going forward, he expects that nonfarm payrolls will increase by about 100K-125K per month in the next six months, which approximates the replacement rate, "more or less signaling stability," he said. By the end of the year, Flack Metals expects the unemployment rate to rise to 4.6% or 4.7%.

That means that unemployment could increase at the same time that tariffs contribute to inflation. Normally, the Federal Reserve would cut rates when unemployment rises, but Doderer thinks "the risk to inflation is going to supersede that typical dynamic." He's forecasting that the Fed won't cut its policy rate for the rest of the year. But neither is he expecting a rate increase.

With the markets swinging down and uncertainty over the tariffs looming, does it even matter what happened in March? Doderer thinks it makes the March data all that much more important. "This is going to be one of the first hard data points that could really be impacted by the uncertainty that has happened over the last couple of months," he said.

data.title: Russell 2000 small-caps gauge hits bear market as tariff fears ripple through stocks
data.description:
The Russell 2000 (RTY) sank into a bear market Thursday, the first key gauge of U.S. stocks to drop at least 20% from its most-recent closing high, with the Trump administration’s plan for global import tariffs triggering a mauling in the equity market.  

The Russell 2000 (RTY) dropped 5% Thursday afternoon. It punched an intraday low of 1,909.70, marking a 21% drop from its closing high of 2,426.56 in early December. The large-cap S&P 500 Index (SP500) fell into correction territory after U.S. President Donald Trump late Wednesday outlined a raft of reciprocal tariffs that will be put on products coming into the U.S. from countries worldwide. 

“The tariff announcements “is a significant negative for the U.S. economy and equities,” Eric Johnston, chief equity and macro strategist at Cantor Fitzgerald, said in a Thursday note. The “10% across-the-board tariff appears to not be negotiable and will be in place for long term and will start April 5,” he said. 

Small-cap stocks rallied after the November election of Trump and the Republican Party back into power in Washington. Investors broadly looked to potential tax cuts and looser regulatory rules to further bolster U.S. economic growth. But recession fears have cropped up with the administration’s ambition to slap levies on trading partners to remedy what it says are problems with trade imbalances and national security, among other issues.

“Recession odds have gone up significantly. We believe [odds] are certainly north of 50%,” and when recessions occur, “earnings almost always go down year over year,” Johnston said. 

The $63.6B iShares Russell 2000 ETF (NYSEARCA:IWM) dropped more than 5% during Thursday’s session. Below are how its 10-largest holdings were moving through midday Thursday: 

  • Sprouts (SFM): -3.3%
  • Insmed (INSM): down less than -0.1%
  • FTAI Aviation (FTAI): -12.7%
  • SouthState (SSB): -9.3%
  • Applied Industrial Technologies (AIT): -8.5%
  • Carpenter Tech (CRS): -9.7%
  • Mueller Industries (MLI): -6.1%
  • Mr. Cooper Group (COOP): -3.6%
  • Halozyme (HALO): -2.1%
  • Beacon Roofing (BECN): -0.1%

Other small-cap exchange-traded funds such as the Vanguard Small Cap ETF (VB) and the Dimensional U.S. Small Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:DFAS) were thrown lower as well on Thursday. 

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Apparel stocks crater as harsh tariffs hit Southeast Asian manufacturing
data.description:
The apparel sector is underwater on Friday as heavy tariffs on merchandise manufactured in Southeast Asian countries like Cambodia, Vietnam, and Sri Lanka are hit with tariffs of 30% to as much as 49%.

To mitigate the damage from the deteriorating relationship between Beijing and Washington, many U.S. companies migrated production to countries in Southeast Asia 6 years ago to capitalize on cheap labor and inexpensive textile costs.

But now, faced with crippling tariffs ranging from 32% for Indonesia, 37% from Bangladesh, 44% from Sri Lanka, and 46% from Vietnam, these companies will need to pass these costs along “in one form or another since they cannot reposition their sourcing,” said Morgan Stanley’s Simeon Gutman, a strategy that could backfire as consumers tighten their belts.

While most apparel manufacturers have some exposure to Southeast Asian suppliers, those with the heaviest reliance on manufacturing in the region include V.F. Corp (NYSE:VFC) with more than half its merchandise made in Vietnam and Indonesia. Gap (GAP) makes 29% of its apparel in Vietnam and another 18% in Indonesia, while Vietnam is responsible for 35% of Skechers’ (SKX) footwear. Swiss-based On Holdings (ONON) makes all of their popular sneakers in Southeast Asia with 90% made in Vietnam and 10% in Indonesia.

 As a result, investors are bailing out of these stocks, leaving V.F. Corp (NYSE:VFC) down 27%, Gap (GAP) down 20%, Skechers (SKX) down 16%, and On Holdings (ONON) down 15. Other notable losers in the category include: Five Below (FIVE) down 26%, Urban Outfitters (URBN) down 18%, Academy Sports and Outdoors (ASO) down 17%, and Boot Barn (BOOT) down 15%.

data.title: Playing defense: Kroger, General Mills, and PepsiCo are notable gainers amid the tariff turmoil
data.description:
Investors turned defensive on Thursday by snacking on grocery, packaged food and beverage stocks. The food industry has less of a direct impact from tariffs than many other industries, although it is still sensitive to economic downturns.

In the grocery store sector, Kroger (KR) was one of the top gainers, with a swing higher of 4.9%. Albertsons Companies (ACI) was up 3.0% and Grocery Outlet Holding (GO) gained 2.1%.

The top packaged food gainer in afternoon trading was Lamb Weston (LW) +9.7% after its earnings report calmed investors' nerves. Other notable advancers included Pilgrim's Pride (PPC) +4.7%, Seneca Foods (SENEA) +3.4%, General Mills (GIS) +3.0%, Kraft Heinz (KHC) +2.2%, and Hormel Foods (HRL) +2.1%. 

In the beverage sector, Coca-Cola (KO) +2.3% and PepsiCo (PEP) +1.7% continued their tradition of holding up on down market days.

The market action fired off when President Trump announced the U.S. will levy tariffs at "approximately half" of what the White House has calculated those countries charge for American goods. The U.S. will place a 34% tariff rate on goods from China, 46% rate on Vietnam, 49% on Cambodia, 25% on South Korea, 31% on Switzerland, 32% on Taiwan, 36% on Thailand, 20% on the European Union, 24% on Malaysia, 10% on the U.K. and Brazil, and 24% for Japan. Trump contended that the tariffs will increase competition and bring down prices, although many economists have warned that inflation and recession risks are now increased.

The question for investors will be if the eye-watering tariffs on a country-by-country basis are just a negotiation tactic, or if the defensive posturing into the food and beverage sector could be a multi-year event if there is deglobalization.
